narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on academic and background factors from past students. The model estimated a 79.3% chance that you would fail the bar exam, which is high enough to lead to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting those predicted to succeed. The two biggest factors pushing the prediction toward failure were your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1st (far below the typical 5th or 6th decile) and your Year 1 decile ranking of 1st (also well below average), signaling to the model that your undergraduate grades placed you in the lowest performance group compared to peers. These early and later academic standings stood out as the strongest influences, outweighing other details and tipping the scales heavily against passing. On a more positive note, your upper-middle family income provided some counterbalance, nudging the prediction slightly toward success since students from similar backgrounds often fare better. Your LSAT score of 29 (below the average of about 37) and undergraduate GPA of 2.90 (under the typical 3.21) also played roles, though they weren't the top drivers here. As a full-time student like most applicants, that aspect aligned with the norm and didn't sway things much either. Compared to typical students who pass, your profile shows weaker academic deciles that mirror patterns in those who struggle on the bar. This isn't a judgment on your potential as an Asian male applicant—many from similar backgrounds succeed with stronger early signals—but the model focused on these gaps. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT or boosting your GPA through post-bac courses to improve your decile rankings, then reapply with those updates. You might also seek targeted bar prep resources early or explore schools with holistic reviews beyond predictions. With focused effort on academics, you can shift your profile toward the successful averages and strengthen future applications.
